translation.content: One major shift in the past ten years has been the rapid adoption of anything-goes DJing. What got us there? Upgraded technology, audience appetite for thrills, instant access to music from every corner of the globe: take your pick. To corral the infinite possibilities of our age, glide seamlessly across lanes and be a trusted conduit in a sea of over-saturation takes chops. And few do it with more gusto and charisma in the 2020s than Jyoty. There's a clear reason why she's a node that connects everyone from Kaytranada, Skrillex and Zack Fox to young clubbers finding community and taking the plunge for the first time at one of her Homegrown parties. Her forthright soapboxing for everything from artistic integrity to human rights is equally a shining example of how to use one's platform. Recorded at a raucous Nowadays party just a few weeks ago for maximum freshness, Jyoty's RA.1000 finds room for 122 tracks dotted over the clubbing map (although, when you tally up all the blends, you can probably round up to 150). From aya to Logan Olm, Hardhouse Banton to DJ Babatr, Busy Signal to Baalti, the. You'll also find delicious double splice of Kelela, a callback to You Got Served and a staggeringly unlikely remix of Noir & Haze's "Around" (yep). All in, it's an audacious statement from a DJ who has made borderless selection their calling card ever since her breakout onto the scene in 2019. With a heavy lean on bubbling and the far frontiers of Brazilian club wares, long passages of the set are maze of microgenres and regional movements worth getting lost in. Jyoty's RA.1000 is a reminder not just of her dynamism in the booth, but a reminder to keep ears open and expose their audience to as much of the future as possible, as quickly as possible. And what could be truer to the foundations of DJing than that? – Gabriel Szatan Can you tell us the idea behind this mix? What do you hope listeners will take away from it? It's my recording from my [Sunday night] set at HYPERLINK CLUB Nowadays in June. Normally at an all-nighter I would start around 115 BPM soulful stuff, but people had been partying outdoors all afternoon and evening, and some folks were even there that morning for the second half of Nowadays' Nonstop party. I know because I was there too, so I recognised people who had never gone home! I want people to enjoy themselves and not overthink it; that's why I did that evening anyway. You can hear imperfections and that's okay—it was a perfect night in itself. Is there a particular RA Podcast that has had a significant impact on you? If so, why? I'm going to go with quite a recent one: DJ Travella. I love specialist sets and I love mixes that I could never pull off. Throughout the entire hour, I kept picking my jaw up from the floor. The less I understand things, the more I love them. Can you share a mix that's particularly special to you? What makes it stand out? I'll go with the first answer that comes to mind: DJ Maseo, one third of De La Soul, on an East London rooftop in 2017. The video seems to be taken down at the moment, but it's everything I think of and want when I think of summertime. Just a masterclass and oh so soulful and sweet. Basically the total opposite of what I've just handed in, haha! Whenever I put it on I just smile and feel good inside. I was there in person too, so I can still feel the sun shining on my face that day when I hear that mix. We couldn't help but notice there's a lot of your own music in this set. What's the plan? Yes, I noticed when typing the tracklist I played 14 tracks of mine! I think most people know where I stand on the DJ-to-producer pipeline. I don't believe in producing purely to advance your DJ career. I understand that it's become the norm, but I want DJs to have faith that you can continue to grow as a DJ, if you focus on perfecting that skill. For the last two years I've been playing original songs and edits and remixes I've made in my DJ sets, but I have no intention of ever putting them out. One of my favourite things about clubbing when I was younger was the chase of trying to find a particular track. Sometimes you would go years without ever figuring it out, until you randomly found the track online on Limewire or Kazaa. But the ecstatic thought of knowing that you could potentially hear it in a set was reason enough to attend. I'm releasing two EPs in the autumn, one uptempo, one downtempo. Neither of them are made with DJs in mind. I approached them to learn how to make songs: producing, writing, playing instruments, directing vocalists and so on. The most interesting thing I've learned in this process is that I absolutely love making soulful, slower music. It takes me way longer, I find it way harder and it's much more complicated, and therefore it fulfills me more. I also really want to respect the craft. I see these two discs as a very humble introduction into the world of producing and look forward to getting better at it. Let's see where it goes and I hope people fuck with it. If not, I'll just go cry in a corner and work on it until they do! These piano and vocal lessons ain't cheap honey! In a world where technology is rapidly changing the way we DJ, how do you see the future of DJing and mixing evolving? You know what? I have absolutely no flippin' clue. Seriously. People are using technology in the most innovative ways and creating entirely new sounds which then create entirely new types of parties/ways of gathering people and I'm just here to enjoy and celebrate it all. I'm just happy to be here.
translation.blurb: For RA.1000, a four-hour document of dance floors in the 2020s, from one of modern club music's most passionate advocates.
